title: The King of Physical Media. Plus: The 10 Best Blu-rays of 2024.
format: No review this week. Sean sits down with playwright and actor Tracy Letts, owner of 10,300 discs, to trace how a post-therapy giallo habit became a collection that fills two basement rooms. Then the two trade the year's best releases: forgotten world cinema from boutique labels like Radiance, and the big 4K restorations worth buying a movie you already own for a third time.

segments.heading: The King of Physical Media
segments.note: 10,300 discs and the sickness behind them

films.title: Serpico
films.note: His parents took him to Dirty Harry at six; it was canceled, so he sat front row for Serpico instead. The start of a childhood with no kids' menu.

films.title: Taxi Driver
films.note: The only movie his parents ever pulled him out of, at a drive-in at 10, when Iris takes Travis back to her room and his mother said she was uncomfortable.

films.title: City of Women
films.note: Seen at 13 at UT while his father taught a summer seminar. The Fellini that told him you could break the rules: 'not his best, God knows,' but a mind-blower.

films.title: Contempt
films.note: Monastic era, a partially furnished Chicago studio, a fork for a comb. He did the math on a $29.99 Criterion at Tower Records, bought it, and the collection really began there.

films.title: Christine
films.note: Where he and Tim Simons (now 'Hitmaker,' too big for a physical-media show) became friends, 'because nobody else would talk to us.'

segments.heading: The small labels doing the real digging

films.title: Messiah of Evil
films.note: Radiance Films, his label of the year, is a UK outfit mining overlooked world cinema. This was his favorite release of the year.

films.title: The Hot Spot
films.note: One of Radiance's few American titles, a Dennis Hopper picture.

films.title: Miami Blues
films.note: Radiance. 'A great transfer of the great Miami Blues.'

films.title: The Landlord
films.note: Radiance's edition of early Hal Ashby.

films.title: Panic in Year Zero!
films.note: Ray Milland directs and stars in a post-apocalypse where everyone needs a gun to survive. 'It's bonkers.' Bleakly relevant, per Letts.

films.title: Tony Arzenta
films.note: Radiance's Italian gangster line: Alain Delon as a hitman trying to get out. 'We've seen it before, we'll see it again.'

films.title: Sympathy for the Underdog
films.note: A Kinji Fukasaku yakuza picture; Radiance mining 1960s and '70s Japanese crime films.

films.title: Yakuza Graveyard
films.note: More Fukasaku from Radiance.

films.title: Big Time Gambling Boss
films.note: A Kosaku Yamashita film Letts had never heard of, one of Radiance's first releases and a good tell of what they do: micro-classics passed over by Criterion and Kino.

films.title: Dirty Money
films.note: Canadian International Pictures (via Vinegar Syndrome). The first of Denys Arcand's informal early-'70s crime trilogy: 'what if Ken Loach made a crime film.'

films.title: Réjeanne Padovani
films.note: The middle of Arcand's low-budget crime trilogy on Canadian International Pictures.

films.title: Gina
films.note: The last of the Arcand trilogy. 'Every country has its own cinema and heritage, and some of it we just never had access to.'

films.title: The Bitter Stems
films.note: Flicker Alley, with the Film Noir Foundation: an Argentinian noir he and Carrie watched last year. 'You are digging in the crates, Tracy.'

films.title: El Vampiro Negro
films.note: Flicker Alley's Argentinian remake of M.

films.title: Deep in the Heart
films.note: Fun City Editions, a New York label that manages about one title a year. A 1983 Texas production Sean bought on Letts's recommendation. 'Bizarrely relevant right now.'

films.title: Bad Company
films.note: Fun City's edition of the Robert Benton Western, wanted on Blu-ray for a long time.

films.title: Rancho Deluxe
films.note: Fun City, the Frank Perry picture.

films.title: Smile
films.note: Fun City, Michael Ritchie's beauty-pageant satire.

films.title: Cutter's Way
films.note: Fun City. 'Always on the list of movies you haven't seen that you need to see,' with an all-world John Heard performance.

films.title: Heartbreakers
films.note: Fun City's Bobby Roth film, with Nick Mancuso and Peter Coyote 'back when Peter Coyote meant something in a movie.' A very specific personal point of view.

films.title: Freedom
films.note: In Fun City's Primetime Panic box of '80s TV movies. Joseph Sargent directs a Barbara Turner script; the mother-daughter scenes 'are better than mother-daughter scenes' in most theatrical films.

films.title: Little Darlings
films.note: Cinématographe (a Vinegar Syndrome offshoot) reissuing undistributed studio pictures in sturdy hard boxes. Put it on the shelf and a three-year-old grabs it: 'what is that pink thing?'

films.title: Touch
films.note: Paul Schrader, cited as another Cinématographe rescue of overlooked studio work.

films.title: Red Rock West
films.note: Cinématographe. Sean's pick, the John Dahl neo-noir.

films.title: Goin' South
films.note: Cinématographe. One of the few films Jack Nicholson directed; Mary Steenburgen's first movie. 'A bit over-cranked, but I love Nicholson.'

films.title: Hollywood 90028
films.note: Grindhouse Releasing: one part art-house existential drama, one part slasher, in the vein of Model Shop. Recommended to Sean by filmmaker Arkasha Stevenson; a major genre discovery of the year.

films.title: Death Game
films.note: Grindhouse Releasing. 'Not exactly what you're expecting based on the packaging.' Thumbs up.

films.title: Interrogation
films.note: Second Run, a small British label heavy on Eastern European cinema. Ryszard Bugajski's harrowing film about living in a totalitarian state, in the Hungarian Masters set.

films.title: Tomorrow I'll Wake Up and Scald Myself with Tea
films.note: Second Run: a Czech sci-fi comedy about neo-Nazis who take a time machine back to deliver a hydrogen bomb to Hitler. 'It is hilarious.'

segments.heading: The 4K restorations worth the double-dip
segments.note: buying a movie you already own, again

films.title: Happiness
films.note: Todd Solondz, seen for the first time since its Angelika run. Letts made Wiener-Dog with Solondz; a title people had begged for.

films.title: Pat Garrett and Billy the Kid
films.note: The last great Peckinpah, in 4K, with a Bob Dylan score and a movie yearned for in the format for a long time. RIP Kris Kristofferson.

films.title: Peeping Tom
films.note: Criterion 4K of the movie that, for all intents and purposes, ended Michael Powell's career and became a huge influence on Italian giallo filmmakers.

films.title: McCabe & Mrs. Miller
films.note: The recurring argument for the format: try it on a 24-inch broadcast TV, then in 4K. With Altman, what's happening at the edge of the frame matters, and the pan-and-scan cut lost 40 percent of it.

films.title: Blood and Black Lace
films.note: Arrow's 4K of the Mario Bava giallo, also the title of the film guide that first sent Letts chasing 200-odd gialli after post-therapy rentals at Cinefile. 'A fucking work of art.'

films.title: Invasion of the Body Snatchers
films.note: The Philip Kaufman version, rediscovered, one of Adam Nayman's favorite films. Lost Donald Sutherland this year.

films.title: The Wages of Fear
films.note: BFI's 4K restoration of the Clouzot: the film Friedkin's Sorcerer adapted, even as Friedkin insisted it wasn't based on it.

films.title: Paths of Glory
films.note: Available in 4K from both Eureka and Kino Lorber, the double-dip shopper's dilemma over which transfer and packaging.

films.title: The Third Man
films.note: Sean's favorite film of all time. He skipped the deluxe edition (the one with a pop-up Ferris wheel and a zither score that plays when you open the box), but the transfer is legit.

films.title: The Conversation
films.note: Kino Lorber. The pricey edition ($79.99) ships with a cassette of the score ('what the hell am I going to play this on?'), but the transfer holds up: the waxy 70s look resolves into new clarity.

films.title: High Noon
films.note: Kino Lorber 4K: 'one of the most significant American films ever made.'

films.title: Last Year at Marienbad
films.note: Kino Lorber. The Alain Resnais 'durational mindfuck.' Not for everyone; for those it's for, a good transfer.

films.title: Prime Cut
films.note: Kino Lorber. Letts owns it on Blu-ray.

films.title: North Dallas Forty
films.note: One of the year's Kino Lorber releases.

films.title: Opera
films.note: Severin's 4K of late-period Argento: 'maybe the last great Argento.' Already sold out by the time Sean tried to buy it.

films.title: Seven Samurai
films.note: Reissued in 4K by both Criterion and BFI. Letts's first Criterion DVD; he now owns it across four formats, the running joke of never being done buying the same movie.

films.title: Bug
films.note: William Friedkin's film of Letts's play, now with a 4K restoration. Letts adored Friedkin, who called him out of the blue and did everything he said he would.

films.title: Nowhere
films.note: Criterion's Gregg Araki, an early creative influence on Letts. Araki's 'omnibus tale of slightly disassociated existentialist teens.'

films.title: Trainspotting
films.note: Criterion 4K, which he loves, but the packaging is taller than every other spine on the shelf. 'A real fuck-you to people with shelves. Don't do that anymore, Criterion.'

films.title: The Devils
films.note: A very good BFI DVD, never issued on Blu-ray. A pandemic movie-night favorite, one of his favorite films of all time, though not one he can put on for Carrie.

films.title: Saint Jack
films.note: Peter Bogdanovich, surprisingly already on Blu-ray, with an underrated Ben Gazzara performance. 'That guy was good and stayed good.'

films: Dracula Has Risen from the Grave
A Killing in a Small Town
